industries.name: Fundamental Nuclear & Atomic Research
uses: Named after Dubna, Russia. The longest-lived isotope (268Db, 29 hours) enables atom-at-a-time aqueous chemistry experiments. Studies confirm Db behaves as a heavier tantalum analog but with relativistic deviations in extraction behavior. Produced by bombarding californium-249 with nitrogen-15 (Berkeley) or americium-243 with neon-22 (Dubna).
Longest-lived known isotope: 268Db, t½ = 29 hours. First synthesized: 1968 Dubna / 1970 Berkeley — disputed, both credited.

Research probes the limits of nuclear stability, tests relativistic quantum chemistry predictions in superheavy atoms, and searches for the theoretical island of stability predicted around Z=114, N=184.
All production requires specialized particle accelerators and isotope reactor facilities — this element cannot be made by any other means.

companies.name: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
companies.use: Ghiorso's team at Berkeley produced and characterized element 105 in 1970 using californium-249 + nitrogen-15 fusion. Proposed the name hahnium (later rejected by IUPAC). LBNL's work established the nuclear properties used to confirm the element.

companies.name: JINR Dubna
companies.use: Flerov's group first reported element 105 in 1968 and proposed the name nielsbohrium. IUPAC ultimately awarded the name dubnium in 1997 to honor Dubna's contributions. Dubna continues transactinide chemistry research.

companies.name: Paul Scherrer Institute (PSI)
companies.use: Performed definitive aqueous chemistry of dubnium using the ARCA (Automated Rapid Chemistry Apparatus), showing Db extraction behavior differs from both niobium and tantalum — demonstrating relativistic effects on Group 5 chemistry.
